text: The surgeon removes the large liver cyst to create sufficient working space in the upper abdomen. A retractor is placed under the liver to elevate it, providing access to the kidney beneath.
The surgeon incises the fascia overlying the kidney, beginning the exposure by opening the encasing normal fat and connective tissue.
The surgeon utilizes the fourth arm of the robotic system to assist with retraction, enhancing exposure of the surgical field around the kidney.

After removing the initial layers of fascia, the surgeon identifies the inferior vena cava and the patient's two renal veins. The gonadal vein is visible draining into the vena cava and is dissected downward to expose the ureter located behind it.
The surgeon observes the ureter as it intermittently contracts, then elevates it to expose the anatomical plane beneath the kidney and above the psoas muscle.

The surgeon cauterizes and divides superficial veins to expose the lower renal artery. The lower pole renal artery is identified and dissected out, preparing it for clamping during tumor resection to limit blood loss.

A vessel loop is placed around the renal artery for gentle retraction. The surgeon uses a stapler to safely ligate the lower renal vein, which overlies the main renal artery, ensuring the upper vein remains intact to preserve kidney function.

The surgeon identifies the main renal artery located behind the upper renal vein and dissects it sufficiently to allow for clamping during tumor resection. Following the dissection of the renal hilum, the surgeon focuses on the tumor, opening the connective tissue over the kidney and removing surrounding fat to expose the tumor.

The surgeon retracts the overlying fat upwards to expose the kidney capsule beneath. This reveals the junction between the tumor and normal kidney tissue, as well as the tumor's proximity to the renal hilum. The planned margin of resection around the tumor is marked with cautery on the kidney capsule.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound with picture-in-picture capability to verify the depth of the planned resection margin, ensuring it is sufficient to avoid cutting into the tumor. The tumor is clearly visible and prepared for resection.

The surgeon pre-places sutures around the anticipated defect site on the kidney for closure after tumor removal. Clamps are pre-positioned on the renal arteries to control bleeding during resection. The arteries are then clamped to initiate tumor removal, minimizing the duration of ischemia to preserve kidney function.

The surgeon injects a fluorescent dye, indocyanine green (ICG), and activates a laser light, causing areas with blood flow to illuminate green. The kidney and tumor remain unlit, confirming successful clamping of blood flow and readiness for tumor resection.
The surgeon begins resecting the tumor after placing clamps on the renal artery, carefully excising the tumor with a small margin of normal kidney tissue to ensure complete removal.
The surgeon completes the excision of the tumor, leaving it aside for later retrieval. The defect in the kidney is then closed by suturing the small veins and arteries at the base of the defect.

The surgeon closes the defect in the kidney by placing sutures across the hole and using clips to apply gentle pressure, preventing bleeding. After closure, the clamps are removed, restoring blood flow to the kidney without any bleeding observed. A second layer of clips is applied to each suture for added security.
The excised tumor is placed into a retrieval bag, and hemostatic agents are applied over the surgical field to minimize venous oozing.
The surgeon places a drain over the kidney to manage potential bleeding or urine leakage. The tumor is extracted, and the incision sites are closed, marking the completion of the procedure.
